name: Oni
description: Fearsome demons/ogres in Japanese folklore—from invisible spirits of misfortune to Buddhist Hell's torturers. The word 'oni' means 'hidden,' originally formless. Transformed through Buddhist integration into red/blue figures representing the Five Poisons (desire, hatred, regret, torpor, doubt). Japan's Three Great Yokai: Shuten-dōji, Tamamo-no-Mae, Ōtakemaru. Red oni (aka-oni) = fire/desire; blue oni (ao-oni) = wood/hatred.
appearance: Large humanoid, horns (ox-horn shape), fanged mouth, three fingers/toes, tiger-skin loincloth, iron club (kanabō). Colors: red (fire/desire), blue (wood/hatred), also yellow/green/black representing other poisons.
behavior: Agents of karmic justice under Emma-ō. Punish specific sins: murderers in Reviving Hell, gluttons in Crushing Hell. Abduct women, steal treasure, eat humans. Momotarō defeated them at Onigashima. Shuten-dōji led 1,000 oni from Mount Ōe before being slain by Minamoto no Raikō.
cultural_significance: Moral causation—'onkō suru' (become oni) describes someone whose heart turned evil. Setsubun festival drives them with bean-throwing. Used to discipline children, justify social order. During wartime, 'oni' demonized enemies.
summary: Fearsome demons/ogres in Japanese folklore—from invisible spirits of misfortune to Buddhist Hell's torturers
fun_fact: In some traditions, Oni is said to agent of karmic justice under emma-ō.
seo_description: Fearsome demons/ogres in Japanese folklore—from invisible spirits of misfortune to Buddhist Hell's torturers. The word 'oni' means 'hidden,' originall
source: Japanese folklore, compiled by Kunio Yanagita and Lafcadio Hearn

name: Pixiu
description: Winged hybrid creature, ninth son of Dragon King. Known as Wealth Guardian - consumes gold and silver but cannot expel it. Powerful symbol of wealth attraction and protection.
appearance: Dragon head, lion body, qilin legs, wings on shoulders. Single horn (Tianlu) or double (Bixie). Fierce expression with large fangs.
behavior: Attracts wealth from all directions, guards against evil spirits. Has sealed anus - takes in wealth but never releases it.
cultural_significance: One of five auspicious Chinese animals (with dragon, phoenix, turtle, qilin). Over 2,000 years history. Popular in feng shui for wealth.
summary: Winged hybrid creature, ninth son of Dragon King
fun_fact: In some traditions, Pixiu is said to attract wealth from all directions, guards against evil spirits.
seo_description: Winged hybrid creature, ninth son of Dragon King. Known as Wealth Guardian - consumes gold and silver but cannot expel it. Powerful symbol of wealth a
source: Chinese mythology, Classic of Mountains and Seas (Shan Hai Jing)

name: Qilin
description: The Qilin is a celestial chimera of the highest order, a creature of such profound purity and benevolence that it refuses to harm even a single blade of grass. In Chinese cosmology, the Qilin is the ultimate herald of the Golden Age, appearing only during the reign of a truly virtuous leader or to announce the birth of a great sage. It represents the perfect harmony of strength and gentleness, a living bridge between the divine heavens and the mortal earth.
appearance: A majestic fusion of forms: the head of a dragon, the antlers of a stag, the scaled body of a carp, and the hooves of a horse. Its skin is a shimmering mosaic of gold and jade, and its mane is a flowing river of celestial fire. It does not walk upon the ground, but glides upon clouds of iridescent mist, its every step accompanied by the soft chime of heavenly bells.
behavior: The Qilin is the embodiment of cosmic peace. It spends its existence wandering the celestial realms and the purest corners of the world, avoiding conflict and suffering. When it descends to the human world, it does so as a silent witness to virtue. It is said that a Qilin will approach a child of destiny with a gentle nuzzle, granting them a lifelong blessing of wisdom and prosperity that can never be stolen.
cultural_significance: One of the four most sacred Chinese animals. Associated with wisdom, prosperity, and benevolent rule.
summary: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader
fun_fact: In some traditions, Qilin is said to be gentle, peaceful, never harms insects or grass.
seo_description: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader. 'Giraffe' in modern Chinese.
source: Chinese mythology, Classic of Mountains and Seas (Shan Hai Jing)

name: Rokurokubi
description: The Rokurokubi are the 'Long-Necked' haunting of Japanese folklore, entities of repressed desire and hidden secrets. By day, they are indistinguishable from ordinary humans—often elegant women who blend perfectly into society. But at night, their physical form betrays their inner turmoil, as their necks stretch to impossible lengths, allowing their heads to roam independently of their bodies in search of sustenance or secrets.
appearance: During the day, they appear as perfectly normal humans. At night, their necks extend like serpents, flowing through the air with a fluid, unsettling grace. The head remains fully conscious and capable of independent action, while the body remains in a deep, catatonic sleep. Their eyes glow with a faint, nocturnal light, and their movement is silent, as if they are gliding through a dream.
behavior: Most Rokurokubi are not intentionally malevolent; they are often cursed beings who cannot control their transformation. They spend their nights spying on neighbors, licking oil from lamps, or simply exploring the city from the rooftops. However, some are predatory, using their extended reach to whisper terrifying secrets into the ears of sleepers or to drain the vitality of those who discover their true nature.
habitat: Normal human settlements, but transform at night
cultural_significance: Explains sleep disorders and sleepwalking. Warning against的秘密 hidden sins or desires.
summary: Human by day, but at night their necks stretch incredibly long, and heads can detach and fly around
fun_fact: In some traditions, Rokurokubi is said to usually harmless, just causing fear.
seo_description: Human by day, but at night their necks stretch incredibly long, and heads can detach and fly around. Usually women cursed for misdeeds.
source: Japanese folklore, compiled by Kunio Yanagita and Lafcadio Hearn

name: Sang-ngag (Gökhang)
description: Tibetan dragon, not like European ones. Serpentine with long body, no wings, often white or blue. Associated with water and weather. Guardian spirit.
appearance: Long, serpentine dragon with flowing mane, no wings, often white or sky-blue. Beautiful and wise-looking.
behavior: Controls weather, brings rain. Protects Buddhist teachings and holy places. Not aggressive unless provoked.
cultural_significance: Important in Tibetan Buddhism and Bon. Dragon flags ('lung ta') carry prayers to dragons.
summary: Tibetan dragon, not like European ones
fun_fact: In some traditions, Sang-ngag (Gökhang) is said to control weather, brings rain.
seo_description: Tibetan dragon, not like European ones. Serpentine with long body, no wings, often white or blue. Associated with water and weather. Guardian spirit.
source: Tibet mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Shidaidaka
description: Roadside yokai that grows taller the more you look up at it.
appearance: Humanoid shadow growing impossibly tall
behavior: Grows impossibly taller the more viewer looks up; challenging presence
habitat: Roadsides, especially Chūgoku region (Tottori, Shimane, Yamaguchi)
cultural_significance: Chūgoku region folklore; perceptual illusion theme
summary: Roadside yokai that grows taller the more you look up at it
fun_fact: In some traditions, Shidaidaka is said to grow impossibly taller the more viewer looks up; challenging presence.

name: Shuten-dōji
description: Leader of oni in Mt. Ōe. The most powerful oni in Japan, feared as one of Japan's 'Three Great Evil Yokai' (日本三大悪妖怪) alongside Tamamo-no-Mae and Emperor Sutoku. His name 'Shuten' means 'sake drinker' - he's called the 'King of Drinking' for his legendary love of alcohol. He commanded hundreds of oni from his mountain stronghold, kidnapping young women from Kyoto to serve as his concubines or be devoured at his gruesome banquets. In the 14th-century Ōeyama Ekotoba scroll, he was identified by diviner Abe no Seimei as the culprit behind the disappearances. The Emperor commissioned Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raikō) to defeat him—disguised as yamabushi (mountain priests), they offered demon-poison sake (jinben kidokusake) given by gods, and beheaded Shuten-dōji during a night raid. Remarkably, his severed head still lived and tried to bite Yorimitsu—only prevented by the hero wearing multiple helmets.
appearance: Giant oni, over 3 meters tall. Red or pale white-blue skin. Often depicted with multiple horns, wild hair, and fearsome expression. Wears ornate kimono or armor when in human form. In true demonic form: red or blue skin, sharp claws, fierce horns, sometimes multiple arms. His child-like moniker (dōji) contrasts with his enormous, terrifying form. Often shown holding sake cup—drunk before his defeat. Famous imagery shows him with disheveled hair, large eyes, and fangs—both juvenile and monstrous.
behavior: Rules over vast oni horde from mountain stronghold. Abducts noble daughters from Kyoto, keeping them captive in his Iron Palace (Onigajō). At banquets, serves 'blood wine' (blood drained from captives) and human flesh as delicacies. Indulges in extreme drinking and luxury. Nearly invulnerable due to supernatural power. Weakness: divine sake poisoned by the gods can incapacitate him. Commands subordinates including Ibaraki-dōji and his Four Divine Kings. Famous last words: 'Oni wa yabazu' (Oni do not lie, cheat, or stray from their path)—embodying his absolute honesty even in defeat.
habitat: Mt. Oe (Ōe Mountains), stronghold called Onigajō (Demon Castle)
cultural_significance: One of the most celebrated figures in Japanese folklore. Featured in the seminal 14th-century picture scroll Ōeyama Ekotoba. Adapted into Noh theater as the play 'Ōe,' which influenced generations of dramatic works. Major subject of Kabuki and Bunraku theater. Ukiyo-e masters including Utagawa Kuniyoshi and Tsukioka Yoshitoshi created famous prints depicting the battle. Among the 23 most popular otogi zōshi tales in the Edo period. Represents the triumph of warrior values over chaotic demonic forces, and the danger of intoxication. His story reflects medieval anxieties about mountain outlaws and the supernatural.

name: Sonangi
description: Jeju Island sea goddess. Woman with long hair controlling tides and storms. Protects fishermen but can sink boats. Important in Jeju fishing culture.

behavior: Controls tides and storms, protects or sinks boats. Sea goddess.
cultural_significance: Jeju sea goddess. Fishing culture importance.

name: Tengu
description: Mountain goblins with long noses and supernatural strength. Protection spirits of forests and mountains, but can be mischievous or dangerous.
appearance: Humanoid with very long red nose, often with bird-like wings or feathers, wearing mountain ascetic clothing (yamabushi).
behavior: Pranksters who can possess humans, but also protect Buddhist law. Teachers of martial arts to worthy students.
cultural_significance: Ambivalent - feared and respected. Bridge between Shinto nature spirits and Buddhist concepts.
summary: Mountain goblins with long noses and supernatural strength

name: Tibetan Snow Leopard Spirit
description: Sacred snow leopard as spirit being. Not just animal but guardian of high mountains. Can shapeshift or be ridden by deities.
appearance: Giant snow leopard, ghostly white or grey-blue. Sometimes appears larger than life, glowing.
behavior: Elusive, protective of mountain balance. Symbol of nobility and solitude. Sometimes helps or tests humans.
cultural_significance: Snow leopard is sacred in Tibetan culture. Represents purity, wildness, and spiritual insight.
summary: Sacred snow leopard as spirit being
fun_fact: In some traditions, Tibetan Snow Leopard Spirit is said to elusive, protective of mountain balance.

name: Tsuchinoko
description: Snake-like cryptid with very thick body and thin tail. Known for fondness for alcohol. Has viper-like fangs and potent venom.
appearance: Very thick snake body, thin tail. Cute but venomous. May look like fat snake.
behavior: Fond of alcohol. Despite silly appearance, has dangerous venom. Many sighting reports - whether real or legendary.
cultural_significance: Popular Japanese cryptid. Surge in sightings throughout Japan. Debate on species vs legend.

name: Tsukumogami
description: Tools and objects that gain sentience after 100 years. Include lanterns, umbrellas, armor - anything that endures.
appearance: Depends on object - could be an animated umbrella with one eye, a floating lantern, a walking armor suit, etc.
behavior: Vengeful if discarded or mistreated. Can be helpful if treated with respect. Often mischievous or troublesome.
cultural_significance: Animism in folk belief. 'Everything has a spirit' - objects develop souls over time and desire continued use.
summary: Tools and objects that gain sentience after 100 years
fun_fact: In some traditions, Tsukumogami is said to vengeful if discarded or mistreated.

name: Yagwanggwi
description: Shoe-Stealing Ghost descending from heavens on Lunar New Year's Eve. Wanders stealing shoes - shoes represent destiny and luck.
appearance: Ghostly form, appears on Lunar New Year's Eve. Obsessed with counting.
behavior: Steals shoes left outside. Losing shoes means losing fortune. Vulnerable to obsession with counting - hang sieves to keep it occupied.
cultural_significance: Korean New Year tradition. Shoes represent Palja (destiny/luck). Unique Korean ghost tradition.
summary: Shoe-Stealing Ghost descending from heavens on Lunar New Year's Eve
fun_fact: In some traditions, Yagwanggwi is said to steal shoes left outside.

name: Yuki-onna
description: A snow woman who appears during blizzards, freezing travelers to death. Sometimes falls in love with humans and can be gentle.
appearance: Tall, beautiful woman in white kimono, pale blue skin, long black hair. Blonde hair described as like snow. Can appear to be floating.
behavior: Appears in snowstorms, blows freezing breath that kills. Can be beautiful and compassionate, but rejects warm love. Must kill anyone she falls for.
cultural_significance: Personification of winter's deadly beauty. Represents emotional coldness and unrequited love.
summary: A snow woman who appears during blizzards, freezing travelers to death
fun_fact: In some traditions, Yuki-onna is said to appear in snowstorms, blows freezing breath that kills.
